Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This year’s exam, the first of the revised study design, offered students the opportunity to engage with tasks related to reading, writing and analysis. As anticipated and foreshadowed by the change in the published assessment criteria and expected qualities, students achieved high standards this year. Most students were well prepared in their understanding of the requirements of the tasks and their skills to complete the three sections of the exam. The teachers and students of the 2024 cohort are to be congratulated both on the way in which students approached the exam and on the skills they were able to demonstrate in the exam.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The paper was designed to offer multiple entry points to students. This offered all students the opportunity to demonstrate their capabilities.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The paper was assessed using the assessment criteria and expected qualities (EQs). The marking was norm-referenced and criteria-based. The marking is holistic as each of the three EQs are connected. For example, the complexity of ideas that a response discusses (first EQ) impacts on the cognitive load carried into subsequent EQs and therefore the ‘complete picture’ needs to be taken into account in order to correctly determine the skill level demonstrated by the response.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: While students generally understood the layout of the paper well, there were some administrative points to note.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students need to remember to nominate a text and topic in Section A and/or write their nominated topic at the beginning of their work.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In Section A, two topics are offered to students. Students are encouraged to select the topic that will best allow them to demonstrate their knowledge and skills.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students need to remember to nominate a Framework of Ideas and/or write their nominated title at the beginning of their response to Section B.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: It is important to note that selecting a title in Section B means that the corresponding Framework has been selected. For example, using the title ‘Resist and Persist’ meant that ideas associated with the Framework of Ideas of Protest must be discussed. Therefore a text that was a transcript of a motivational half-time address by a coach during a sports grand final, while relating to the title, would not have been presenting ideas related to the appropriate Framework (Protest) for a specific purpose and would not meet the requirements of the task. Students could usefully spend exam preparation time developing strategies to explore relevant ideas in relation to unseen topics, unseen titles and stimuli and unseen texts for analysis.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Section A: Analytical response to a text

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were offered a proposition in relation to ideas/values presented in a text. Students were invited to challenge the proposition where warranted. The invitation was framed by one of the following instructions:
Discuss.
To what extent do you agree?
Do you agree?

Examples: In Pride and Prejudice, life is easier when individuals conform to social conventions.
To what extent do you agree?
False Claims of Colonial Thieves suggests that, despite their differences, people should seek to understand each other.
Discuss.
In High Ground, a desire for control is the most important factor in decision-making.
Do you agree?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were offered a quotation as well as an instruction/proposition. The quotation related to the topic and thus the convention was that the student would discuss the way in which the quotation was addressing a key idea in the topic as part of their answer.
It is interesting to note that nearly half the topics fell into this category.

Examples: ‘There’s nothing tragic about being 50, not unless you try to be 25.’
In Sunset Boulevard how does Wilder portray Hollywood’s infatuation with youth?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
Students are invited to answer a direct question about the text.

Examples: To what extent is deception harmful in Much Ado About Nothing?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Another way of cataloguing these topics was to consider the invitation they offered students. This may include, but is not restricted to, topics that:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
A topic might ask students to consider two or more concepts and the validity of a specified relationship between the terms.
Responses can challenge the relationship but must address the ideas presented by the author in relation to the nominated concepts.

Example: ‘Tell her your story – I mean your own story.’
Requiem for a Beast suggests that shared stories are vital for connecting people and communities.
Discuss.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Drill into a specific aspect of a concept presented by the text
SectionHeaderContent:
A topic might ask students to ‘drill into’ a concept presented in a text and discuss one (or more) specified aspect(s) of the concept.

Example: In Oedipus the King all who deserve it are justly punished.
Do you agree?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Consideration of concept/idea development in the text
SectionHeaderContent:
Students might be asked how a concept/idea was explored in a text both by discussing the way ideas were introduced and developed and by exploring the way in which they were conveyed by the author.

Example: How does Ottley explore the power of both guilt and forgiveness?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Please note there is no definitive list of verbs used by the examination panel to explore the way in which meaning is conveyed by an author and new terms are regularly introduced. Students would do well to develop their understanding of such terms. Terms are often nominalised in topics and students should be prepared for this.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Or the use of a linking verb to imply there is a direct connection by use of is/are or is not/are not

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students who can demonstrate an understanding of the implications of the topics will typically develop responses that achieve higher scores. There are often multiple implications in a topic. The following table outlines some strategies used in essay topics in 2024.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: By the use of absolute terms
SectionHeaderContent:
‘No one in My Brilliant Career is ever truly satisfied.’
Do you agree?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: By the connotations of verbs, nouns or adjectives
SectionHeaderContent: How does Johnson explore different perceptions of truth in High Ground?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: By the ‘silence’ in a proposition (or quote)
SectionHeaderContent:
‘Why should a man suffer so grievously for an offence he had committed inadvertently?’
In Things Fall Apart the consequences of actions are rarely fair.
Do you agree?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
‘You can trust me. I am ready to help.’
In the play, the desire to behave honourably causes suffering.
To what extent do you agree?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: By the inclusion of a quote
SectionHeaderContent:
‘Paradise is beneath your mother’s feet’
How does Erpenbeck explore what it means to belong?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Scripts were assessed holistically using the published EQs. Benchmarks were used to establish the standard required in each of three interrelated skills:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: the capacity to create a reading of the text’s ideas/values

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: the capacity to sequence and substantiate ideas relevant to the topic

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: the capacity to communicate these ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were assessed on the skills they exhibited. The following are excerpts of high-scoring responses.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In Units 3 and 4 students explored one of the four Frameworks of Ideas and associated mentor texts on the 2024 VCE English and English as an Additional Language (EAL) text list. The exam invited students to create a text that responded to a nominated title, at least one of the three pieces of stimulus material provided and explored meaningful ideas associated with the selected Framework of Ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This text needed to establish and maintain an appropriate voice to achieve a purpose: to explain, express, reflect or argue. Students were invited to create a cohesive text that demonstrated their capacity to explore relevant ideas through purposeful authorial decisions relating to text structure(s), language features and vocabulary.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As with other sections of the paper, the titles provided multiple entry points and potential directions for students to follow.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The stimulus material was provided to help students generate relevant ideas in relation to the nominated titles.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: There was no preferred VCAA style or content.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Mentor texts did not need to be referenced.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were free to explore the ideas associated with the title and the Framework of Ideas in any prose style.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As in other sections of the course, students needed to draw on their knowledge of the centrality of context, audience and purpose to successful text creation.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: While students may have created their text for an intended audience (such as like-minded protestors, a young adult reader or a more private self-directed audience), it was important to remember that there was a secondary audience to consider.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The text formed part of a public examination and could (with student permission) be published on the VCAA website, which has a broad readership.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The student’s text will also be read, for the purposes of assessment, by an unknown adult.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As such, students are encouraged to consider the text they create in terms of appropriateness for their intended audience and purpose, as well as this secondary audience and purpose.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The text’s purpose(s) must be those nominated in the VCE English and English as an Additional Language Study Design.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Consideration of the implications of these factors should form part of students’ preparation for the exam.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Scripts were assessed holistically using the published EQs. Benchmarks were used to establish the standard required in each of three interrelated skills. These were:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: quality of Framework ideas that connect to the title and stimulus

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: capacity to develop and explore ideas, using voice to achieve their purpose

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: capacity to communicate ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students responded to this section of the exam confidently. Clear authorial voices were established and the writing was strong.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: High-scoring responses showed thoughtful engagement with relevant ideas and demonstrated skill in presenting and developing texts for a specific purpose, and were presented in each of the four Frameworks of Ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were assessed on the skills they exhibited. The following are annotated examples of mid- to high- scoring responses. Please note, the ideas presented are those of the student and are not necessarily endorsed by their schools or the VCAA.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The student uses the title as a lens and the opportunity to create a metaphor about the connections needed within the fabric of a community to preserve country.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The image of the grandfather guiding a child through a crop in stimulus 2 is used as a recurring motif throughout the piece.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The length is approximately 900 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The title was explored by a consideration of factors against which resistance is necessary and the reasons why the vigilance has been or is needed in human society.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The connection to stimulus 2 was implied by the text – factors that precipitated protest and, by implication, ways to control such a situation.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The length is approximately 600 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The title was explored by a consideration of new forms of resistance. The persistence of injustice was used as a warning of what injustices may be prolonged without resistance.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The metaphor of stimulus 2 was used as a motif throughout the text.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The length is approximately 900 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The title was explored through an explanation of what needs to be resisted, and these acts of protest are resisted by society.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The imagery of the dominoes falling was used as a repeated motif of the cascading impact of social injustice that requires resistance from responsible members of society.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The length is approximately 1000 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The writer explored the title by exploring the stages of developing a sense of identity.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The idea of transformation to a state of ‘beauty’ of completeness from stimulus 3 was used as a structural mechanism.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The length is approximately 1000 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The student has used the title to explore the complex journey of navigating close relationships.

SectionHeaderContent: Stimulus 3 was selected and the student referenced ideas of transformation through their evolving sense of identity.

SectionHeaderContent: The length is approximately 1000 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The student used the title in two ways. There was consideration of the way the value of play can be subverted at a time when society believes it is the appropriate time to play. Then this idea was developed at the end where the experience of this time reshaped an understanding of life.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The student referenced both stimulus 1 and 2 ironically by exploring the consequences of not having freedom to play and not having the outcome of play prescribed and limited.

SectionHeaderContent: The title was explored through this piece by its emphasis on the developmental importance of play in childhood.

SectionHeaderContent: The student used both stimulus 1 and 2 for inspiration. The text clearly uses the idea of free play and the figurative idea of the child’s mind that is powered by play.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Section C: Analysis of argument and language

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were invited to ‘write an analysis of the ways in which arguments, written and spoken language and visuals were used … to persuade the intended audience to share a point of view.’ As with the other sections of the exam, this section was assessed holistically using the assessment criteria and expected qualities.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Benchmarks were used to establish the standard required in each of three interrelated skills.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: These were:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: an analysis of argument:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: - the capacity to explain strategic sequencing of ideas to persuade

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: an analysis of language:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: - the capacity to explain the role of written and visual language in presenting and developing the argument

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: - the capacity to explain the ways argument and language function together to persuade the intended audience

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: the capacity to communicate these ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The ‘Background Information’ provided crucial information for the completion of the task.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: It was here that the students were given the context in which the text was created and the identity of the text’s author and the intended audience was defined.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students needed to use this knowledge as well as inferred knowledge during their reading of the previously unseen persuasive text to establish the author’s purpose, the characteristics of the intended audience and the relationship between the author and the intended audience.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This information derived by inferential reading was vital for students as they demonstrated their skill in analytical thinking while completing the task.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: There was no preferred structure or content.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were free to adopt their own strategy to explain the ways in which the text was structured to persuade the intended audience and the ways in which language and visual language were harnessed to convey and amplify the ideas presented.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In 2024, the students were presented with a previously unseen text about a fictional gym experiencing financial insecurity and considering a partnership with another business, which held apparently conflicting values, to secure its financial viability.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The form was a speech made to an audience of Our Local Gym members presented at an emergency meeting.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Two slides were shown to the audience during the speech.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The Chair of the gym was Sandy Gestalt, whose purpose was to persuade the gym’s members to put aside their values for pragmatic reasons and vote in favour of the controversial partnership.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Her contention was that the values of the two businesses could be reconciled by reframing perceptions, and that such a compromise was a financial necessity.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As in previous sections of the exam, the task was designed to allow students multiple entry points so that they could demonstrate their skills to the best of their ability.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: One way of representing the line of argument is as follows:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Remind the audience of both their connections and vulnerability.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Highlight the value of what is at risk.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Present the likelihood of loss.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Offer a solution as the only possible ‘lifeline’.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Rebut possible objections to undermine backlash.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Reframe the situation as ‘an opportunity’.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Apply moral pressure and present an image of a rosy future before asking members to vote on the proposal.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were not expected to identify each stage of the argument in minute detail. It was up to the student to select elements of the text to demonstrate their analytical capacity.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Some strategic language choices included language used to:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: strategically characterise key stakeholders, for example, ‘obnoxiously loud’, ‘safe space’, ‘they offer’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: encompass the community, but also distinguish between ‘lesser’ gyms, and to present collective responsibility through the use of pronouns and other strategies

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: appeal to the audience’s values, for example, ‘you have built your fitness’, ‘we are a community’, ‘they understand our’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: highlight dichotomies, for example, ‘selfies on their socials’ versus ‘all bodies and all fitness journeys’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: lighten the mood to imply the issue was not serious, for example, ‘worked together / worked out together’, ‘sweet delight to work with’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: minimise the moral dilemma, for example, ‘uncomfortable’, ‘alternate funding’, ‘limited nutritional benefit’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: escalate emotional responses, for example, ‘dire’, ‘bleak’, ‘closing … entirely’, ‘a carpark’, ‘be demolished!’, ‘lifeline’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: reassure, for example, ‘guarantee’, ‘an opportunity’, ‘allow our imperfect community to flourish’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: imply one option, for example, ‘only viable solution’, ‘a lifeline’, ‘we are voting for many’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: reframe the dubious association, for example, lollies bring ‘joy’, ‘fun and laughter are … important … to health’, ‘our imperfect community’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: pivot from the bleak to the optimistic, for example, ‘But we have found another option. A lifeline.’

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Some strategic visual cues used in the images included:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: employing figurative associations

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: establishing atmosphere

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: using slogans

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: using symbols

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: giving a representation of gym members.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Students were not expected to laboriously identify and discuss every element. They were expected to select what was most pertinent to the persuasion to demonstrate their analytical capacity.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Students were also invited to discuss the interplay between argument, language and visual elements of the text.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Section A: Analytical response to a text

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In Section A, students were asked about the ideas and associated values that an author presented in a selected text. The tasks were not presented as broad prompts to instigate the student’s own wide-ranging consideration of the text; instead, they were narrow topics about specified ideas and values the author presented in relation to these concerns and associated consequences, implications or connections. This had implications for the:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: ways students structured their responses

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: ways students sequenced their responses

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: types of textual evidence students used to support their responses to examination topics

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: conclusions students drew in relation to their reading.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In Units 3 and 4, students explored two of the 20 texts on the VCE English and English as an Additional Language (EAL) Text List 2025. The examination invited students to write an essay of analysis on one of these texts. For each text, there was a choice of two topics. These topics invited students to consider the ideas and/or values that the author presented in relation to the topic.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As with other sections, Section A was assessed using the published assessment criteria and expected qualities (EQs). The marking was norm-referenced and criteria-based. Holistic assessment practices were used as each of the three EQs are interconnected.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The first of the EQs in each section referenced the quality of the student’s ideas. The complexity of ideas that the student presented then set the level of challenge they faced in EQ2, which was about the way the ideas were structured, elaborated and substantiated. EQ3 was about the student’s proficiency with language and capacity to communicate their selected ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: EQs are always applied in relation to the topic. For example, EQ1 in Section A was applied in relation to the ideas that the student presented as a response to the chosen topic. A student presenting sound ideas that did not relate to the topic could not score highly. If the student used textual evidence, such as quotations that did not pertain to a relevant discussion, or if a student demonstrated proficiency in language but the content of their response did not relate to the specified topic, the EQ relating to language was not applied independently of the other two criteria. Students were assessed on the textual knowledge, capacity to explain the connections between the ideas they presented, and the language skills they demonstrated in relation to completing the set task.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As always, Section A offered multiple entry points for students. Every student had the opportunity to use one entry point to demonstrate the skills they had developed.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students who had topic interpretation strategies were well-prepared to respond to topics that were presented in both familiar and unfamiliar ways.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were offered a proposition in relation to ideas/values presented in a text. Students were invited to challenge the proposition where warranted.
The conceptual focus/foci of a topic cannot be challenged or dismissed. For example, a discussion of Robbie Arnott’s Flames, ‘Love and destruction are inseparable in Flames’, should have included a discussion about the connection between ‘love’ and ‘destruction’. However, challenging the relationship between them as ‘inseparable’ was also appropriate.
Topics were framed by one of the following instructions:
Discuss.
Do you agree?
To what extent do you agree?
Students who understood the difference between these command terms were better able to address these propositional essay questions.

Examples: Love and destruction are inseparable in Flames.
Discuss.
In Oedipus the King, there are no right choices.
Do you agree?
In Born a Crime, women exert the most influence on Noah’s life.
To what extent do you agree?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were offered a quotation as well as an instruction/proposition. The quotation related to the topic and thus the convention was the student would discuss the way in which the quotation addressed a key idea in the topic as part of their answer. For example, in Jane Eyre by Charlotte Brontë, Jane’s passionate declaration to Rochester established her ability to determine her own fate. 
The metaphor used by the character referenced social power imbalances. Considered in its context, the quotation also conveyed ideas about Jane’s moral agency, which related directly to her capacity to actively pursue personal freedom from individuals, religion and society. The way in which students used the quotation reflected their understanding of the meaning it conveyed.
Students were not obligated to use the quotation in a specific way. They were free to integrate it into their response in a way that related to the reading they presented.
Quotation questions contained a proposition and one of three command terms, which were followed by a direct question. Students needed to understand the conventions associated with addressing other types of questions to be well prepared for this section.

Examples: ‘I am no bird; and no net ensnares me …’
Jane Eyre is primarily a novel about the pursuit of personal freedom.
Do you agree?
‘Hopeless, homeless, aimless, shameless souls …’
The world of My Brilliant Career is harshly unforgiving.
To what extent do you agree?
‘How can I but take up the call, Charmaine, and yarn right back at you –
it’s what we do when we connect …’
How does False Claims of Colonial Thieves highlight the necessity of solidarity?
‘Blood calls to blood.’
In The Erratics, Laveau-Harvie highlights the tension between family obligation and self-preservation.
Discuss.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were invited to answer a direct question about the text.
Note: While these were the only types of direct questions used in the 2025 examination, there have been different forms of direct questions in previous papers and may be different ones in the future.

Examples: To what extent is revenge a form of justice in High Ground?
How does Bad Dreams and Other Stories depict the consequences of crossing boundaries?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Explore the relationship between concerns/ concepts
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were asked to consider two or more concerns/concepts along with the validity of a specified relationship between these.
Responses could challenge the relationship but must address the ideas presented by the author in relation to the nominated concepts.
The following relationships were used in the 2025 examination:
Concept act as a catalyst for a concept (Question 1ii)
Concept caused by (Question 5ii)
Concept can be related to another concept (Question 15ii)
Concept has differences to (Question 16ii)
Concept is/are/essential directly related to another (Question 6ii)
Consequence of (Question 1i)
Concept caused by (Question 17i)
Concept despite concept (Question 8i)
Concept power over another concept (Question 7ii)
Concept leads to concept (Question19ii)
Concept is more dangerous than concept (Question 9ii)
Concept’s lack of connection to another concept (Question 3ii)
Concept requires more than of concept (Question 13ii)
Concept is primarily something in relation to another concept (Question 1i)
Concept has capacity to coexist with another concept (Question 15ii)

Example: Relationships act as catalysts for self-discovery in Bad Dreams and Other Stories.
Discuss.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Drill into a specific aspect of a concern/concept presented by the text
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were asked to consider a specified aspect of a concept presented in a text and discuss one (or more) specified aspect(s) of the concept. For example, the topic does not invite students to write about Moss’s ideas in relation to glorifying the past but only a specified aspect of this. Students’ responses needed to centre on the dangers of glorifying the past. Focusing on this aspect alone, rather than incorporating it in a wider discussion, is the task’s invitation.

Example: Ghost Wall warns of the danger of glorifying the past.
To what extent do you agree?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Consideration of concept/idea development in the text
SectionHeaderContent:
Students were asked how a concept/idea was explored in a text both by discussing the way ideas were introduced and developed, and by exploring the way in which these ideas were conveyed by the author.

Example: How does Brontë highlight the danger of acting on emotion rather than reason in Jane Eyre?

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The verbs used in topics related to the author’s purpose in exploring ideas. Understanding the meaning of these verbs was an important aspect to students’ understanding of the topic’s invitation. Responses may have implicitly challenged the verb used in the topic, but it would have been unwise to ignore the verb or to conflate all verbs into a generic concept, such as ‘presents’.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: There was no definitive list of verbs used by the assessors to explore the way in which meaning was conveyed by an author, and new terms were introduced. Students would do well to develop their understanding of such terms. Verbs were often nominalised or implied in topics and students should be prepared for this.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Note: The use of linking verbs implied a direct connection by using is/are or is not /are not.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Considering the implications of a topic meant that students understood the complexity of the question. Thus, there was a correlation between students who could demonstrate an understanding of the implications of the topics, and higher scores. There were often multiple implications in a topic.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Responses were assessed holistically using the published assessment criteria and associated EQs. Benchmark student scripts were used to establish the standard required in each of three interrelated skills:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: The capacity to create a reading of the text’s ideas/values.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: The capacity to sequence and substantiate ideas relevant to the topic.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: The capacity to communicate these ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were assessed on the skills they exhibited.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: The following are excerpts from high-scoring responses.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Section B invited students to create one written text that explored meaningful connections with ideas drawn from one of the four Frameworks of Ideas listed in VCE English and English as an Additional Language Study Design: writing about country, protest, personal journeys or play.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students needed to use linguistic features to create a ‘voice’ to connect with the reader in order to facilitate the communication of their ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This response needed to achieve one of the four purposes listed in the study design: to explain, express, reflect or argue.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: It should be noted that responses could achieve more than one purpose.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: While students could select, adapt or subvert any form of prose writing, they needed to use an understanding of context, audience and purpose to create a cohesive text and an exploration of the ideas for their chosen Framework.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were also free to use any structure, linguistic features or vocabulary to establish their voice and achieve their purpose – such authorial decisions played an important role in the way the student formed their response.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The examination task required students to use the title specified for their Framework.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: While there was no preferred method for doing this, the title provided the conceptual parameters of the text that students were invited to create.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As well, students needed to incorporate the Framework of Ideas suggested by one or more (if they chose) piece(s) of stimulus material presented in the examination.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were offered three pieces of stimulus material, including one visual.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Just as students were free to select a purpose, stimulus and form for their writing, they were able to address their writing to any audience – for example, the student body at a school assembly or the recipient of a letter.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: However, this task formed part of an examination, and as such, there is always a secondary audience for this response – the assessor.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were encouraged to keep the examination specifications and published assessment criteria front of mind as they made authorial decisions during the examination.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: Examination responses are not private texts, therefore, thought needed to be given to appropriate content, as an unknown adult would be reading it for the purposes of assessment.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As part of examination preparation, students should be encouraged to use the text’s structure and linguistic choices to connect in powerful ways to the readership.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Sensationalised content, cloying sentiment or use of cliched language, images or tropes were rarely the most powerful option to convey ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Sound examination preparation would empower students with both the skills and agency to avoid such pitfalls.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Points of note:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students are reminded to nominate a framework and stimulus at the beginning of their response to Section B. For example:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent:
Shade the box of the Framework of Ideas you have selected.
 Framework 1: Writing about country
Shade the box of the Stimulus you have selected. 1  2  3

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: It was helpful to the assessor if the student wrote the title at the start of their response.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Selecting a ‘title’ in Section B meant that the corresponding Framework was also selected.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: For example, using the title ‘Origins’ meant that ideas associated with Framework 1: Writing about country must be discussed and the associated stimuli used.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: If a student nominated one Framework but used a different title, the assessor used the title as the guide for the student’s selection.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: For instance, if a student selected Framework 1: Writing about country, but wrote on ‘Small Acts, Big Wins’, the assessor would assume the student had accidently ticked the wrong Framework box.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This strategy assumed that a mistake can be made more easily in selecting a box than in transcribing a title.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students could not ‘mix and match’ titles and frameworks.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The same was true of stimulus material. While students could use any ideas they liked, the ideas had to be drawn from the stimulus material of the selected Framework.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The title was fixed.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students could not change or modify the title.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: If a student did this, no marks were deducted, but the assessor ignored the addition.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Mentor texts do not need to be referenced.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As with other areas of the examination, there was no preferred content, style or form for responses.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Rather, the assessor’s focus was on how the student developed the title and stimulus idea(s) (and others) into and through a cohesive text.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were assessed on the skills they demonstrated.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This was consistent with the approach taken in other areas of the examination.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: As in other sections, Section B was assessed holistically using the published assessment criteria and EQs. Benchmarks were used to establish the standard required in each of three interrelated skills:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: quality of Framework ideas that connected to the title and stimulus

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: capacity to develop and explore ideas, using voice to achieve purpose

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: capacity to communicate ideas through use of linguistic structures.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: As in other sections, the EQs in Section B were strategically sequenced. The relevance and quality of the ideas selected for exploration by the student set the challenge in creating a voice to communicate, develop and explore them, and the linguistic structures and devices that amplified them.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As this is a new area of study, and one that can be completed in many ways, a series of annotated responses are included below to demonstrate and celebrate student success. The annotations indicate factors considered in the assessment of the text but are not an exhaustive or exclusive list of factors considered by assessors. These texts scored in the high-middle and high range.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Note: It must be remembered that this was a timed, first-draft writing by Year 12 students, and perfection was an unrealistic expectation. The range of voices and styles used in the examples do not indicate VCAA-endorsed approaches.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Innovation and individuality were encouraged. Humans will always find new and powerful ways to use language to communicate, even within the confines of an examination. This was embraced.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students used this Framework as an opportunity to explore complex ideas around migration and identity.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Origins’ as an opportunity to explore ideas about the ways in which place of origin can form identity, and how identity might evolve over time.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: The idea of ‘hearts’ caught by country and its associated cultures and understanding, from Stimulus 1, was referenced implicitly and explicitly through using river imagery and figurative associations.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: This response mixed reflection and explanation to present an affirmative vision of how a person’s country of origin shapes and reflects but does not determine their identity and relationship with country throughout their life.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1100 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Origins’ as an opportunity to explore the origins of trauma, its associations with country, and the way in which country can also offer reconciliation and healing through developing feelings of stewardship.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: This narration reflected on a complex relationship between country and its inhabitants, and illustrated how healing relationships with the land can occur over time.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: Reference to Stimulus 3 was inferred through the narrator’s understanding of country shaped by family history and association.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 800 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Origins’ to explore the powerful role of culture associated with country of origin, to offer both identity and agency to individuals.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: Reference to Stimuli 1 and 3 was inferred through the narrator’s understanding of country shaped by family history and association.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: The narrative voice reflected on experience and explained their newfound understanding of country and identity.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 800 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Origins’ to explore how reconciling with country of origin is central to happiness. This response both reflected on and explained meaningful Framework ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: Reference to Stimulus 1 was made by the narrator’s inability to shift their heart until they had acknowledged and paid tribute to the role of country in shaping their identity.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 1: Writing about country
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1100 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This year there were fewer students who mistook the noun in this Framework title for a verb. Students were encouraged to use the description of the Framework found in the study design as their guide for appropriate Framework ideas. Many students were able to use their passion for social justice to inspire their exploration of appropriate Framework ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title, ‘Small Acts, Big Wins’, as an opportunity to explore ideas about what constituted both an act of protest and a win when protesting. The narrator explored what is taught and what is believed, and the forces that allowed ideas to change, develop and adapt.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: The idea of ‘survival as a win when facing violent oppression’ was explored and ultimately challenged. Survival in silence was presented as defeat. It was the survival of stories that was seen as the ‘win’ in a heart-breaking story of coming to terms with oppression and exile.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: This response mixed reflection and explanation to present an affirmative vision of how a person’s stories provided a legacy that restored honour and pride even in the face of devastating injustice.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1300 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title, ‘Small Acts, Big Wins’, to illustrate the benefits of courting and connecting with ‘Protest’ as a partner in life. The text imagined Protest’s profile on a dating site and what it offered prospective partners. The text both explained and presented a case for ‘Protest’.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: The metaphor of Stimulus 2 was used as a motif throughout the text.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 900 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title, ‘Small Acts, Big Wins’, to analyse a photograph. The action of taking a photograph was presented to promote and inspire protest, and the action within the photograph was also shown to be a small act that generated big wins. The student managed to balance the two ideas effectively in this piece seeking to both explain and argue a case for small acts of protest as justified by the big wins they result in.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: The imagery from Stimulus 3 was introduced and developed throughout the text.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1000 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Small Acts, Big Wins’ to promote the work of an international non-governmental organisation and created an argument for the reader to join the group, suggesting that the individual’s actions were part of a larger humanitarian struggle. Thus, the text both explained and presented a case.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: The student used the ideas of Stimulus 3 as the basis of their argument.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 2: Writing about protest
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1000 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Many responses to this task reflected the causes and consequences of changes of direction in lives as well as the role these changes mark in maturity and wisdom.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: This response considered the role of sibling relationships in personal journeys and how these change with time. It used the title ‘Changing Direction’ as an opportunity to also consider the role forgiveness plays in relationships and life’s direction.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: This response referenced the ideas of emergence and development from Stimulus 2 as well as adopting the imagery as a recurring symbol through the text. Stimulus 3 was used as the idea to drive the whole piece.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 600 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: This response considered the changes of direction forced by fate and the impact these have on fundamental beliefs that form identity.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: The text, set during a single church service, explored the way humans can change direction from rage to acceptance. Thus, the title ‘Changing Direction’ was explored.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: Stimulus 3 was the idea that drove the whole response: the implicit reference could not be missed.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 900 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: This response encouraged readers to reflect through the narration on the impact of trauma, the power of guilt and the change that atonement brings to a life’s direction, and thus, the ‘Changing Direction’.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: The student used Stimulus 2 in many ways, imagery of boats morphed as the piece progressed into imagery of cranes, which explored ideas of change. The implied and explicit links to the stimulus were seamlessly integrated into a powerful narrative.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1100 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Changing Direction’ to consider the limitations we set ourselves and considered the changes and options that can appear if we remove these self-imposed restrictions.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: This response used Stimulus 1 and its metaphor throughout the text to make a point about the agency of individuals.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 3: Writing about personal journeys
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1000 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Many students explored the lessons learned via game playing and found parallels with the rules and structures of life in this task. Some students accepted the Framework’s invitation to play with form, language and expectations.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Life is a Game’ as an opportunity to explore ideas about the learning that playing both games and roles offered as preparation for engagement with life.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Stimulus 1 was explored explicitly and the text was an illustration of this idea. This response was elevated by its powerful reflective narrative voice that explored both an individual and contemporary truth. The ending was thoughtful and nuanced in its resolution and the optimism was uplifting. Thus, this student achieved their purposes of both reflecting and persuading.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 600 words. (Note: This response is an excellent example of how an economical text can be effective when exploring complex Framework ideas.)

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: This response explored the life lessons learned from golf, which mimicked the lessons needed to ‘play life successfully’. The student presented a clear explanation of how this could be achieved by referencing a golfing career. This response was a speech given to young golf enthusiasts to highlight the importance of the game.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Stimulus 3 was used as one of those life lessons.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1100 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Life is a Game’ to promote the difference between the triviality of games (which it suggested life had become) and the serious value of play (which it suggested should form the focus of life), presenting the case that the education system (and English assessors in particular!) have lost sight of this important distinction to the detriment of their and others’ humanity.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Simulus 3 was considered at a societal level as well as an individual level.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 1000 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: This response used the title ‘Life is a Game’ to condemn games played as if they were an authentic substitute for life and suggested that a return to more authentic play would lead to a more fulfilling life.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Ideas from Stimulus 1 were referenced throughout this response – not necessarily suggesting that ‘our true selves’ is an ideal state but making the case that sometimes we need to learn something of more value before we can understand the possibilities of being human.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Framework of Ideas 4: Writing about play
SectionHeaderContent: Length: approximately 800 words.

Subject: English
SectionHeader: Section C: Analysis of argument and language

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In Section C, students were asked to analyse the persuasive strategies and devices in a letter to a community newspaper calling for change.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As with the other sections, Section C was assessed holistically using the published assessment criteria and EQs.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Benchmarks were used to establish the standard required in each of three interrelated skills:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: an analysis of argument:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: - the capacity to explain strategic sequencing of ideas to persuade

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: an analysis of language:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: - the capacity to explain the role of written and visual language in presenting and developing an argument designed to persuade the intended audience

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: - the capacity to explain the ways argument and language function together to persuade the intended audience

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: the capacity to communicate these ideas.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As in past examinations, crucial information about the text’s context and audience were given to the students in the ‘background information’.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In this scenario, the stimulus text was published in a community newsletter.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The implication of the publication details, along with the information that this was a regional town, needed to be used by students to infer both the audience and their interests.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The author was presented as a ‘local resident’.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Most students were able to infer from this the author’s altruistic motivation and extrapolate this information to determine a way in which the author established credibility with the intended audience.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Many responses demonstrated an understanding of the text’s purpose  to promote community acceptance of a change in the way the town marked an annual celebration.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: While the details of the change were explored in the text, the timing of the change was left vague.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The text did not present a sense of urgency, rather it presented a proposed alternative as part of an evolution towards a more inclusive society.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This ‘background information’ derived by inferential reading was vital for students as they demonstrated their skill in analytical thinking while completing the task.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: This allowed them to present a more focused and insightful analysis.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: There was no preferred structure or content.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were free to adopt their own strategy to explain the ways in which the text was structured to persuade the intended audience.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: They were also free to select what they felt were the most important ways in which the written language and visuals were harnessed to convey and amplify the ideas presented.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Higher-scoring students were able to explain the ways in which these elements complemented one another to form a cohesive text.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: As in previous sections of the examination, the task was designed to allow students multiple entry points so that they could demonstrate their skills to the best of their ability.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students found the text highly accessible, and most recognised the ideas presented within the text such as:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: strategically establishing a shared value and appreciation of the celebration with the intended audience

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: pointing out previously unconsidered problems: the environmental impact, the impact on pets (including a visual), noise-sensitive humans and cost, in a way that aligned with community values

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: rebutting of reasons to maintain the status quo

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: calling to adopt the alternative as a ‘win-win’ solution (including a visual).

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Most students submitted complete responses for this section.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Students were able to identify the arguments; however, not all students were able to explain how the sequencing of the ideas or the connection between them reflected the text’s purpose.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The photograph of a very cute dog frightened by the noise of the fireworks, engaged most students in one way or another.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Most were able to identify the ways the images presented ideas that related to the argument.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Their capacity to determine the contribution that the images made to the development of the argument was often indicative of their skill level.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Most students were able to identify some of the linguistic techniques used.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: More skilful responses were able to explain how these elements were designed to persuade the intended audience and the role these elements played in the persuasion.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The most successful responses were able to discuss the interplay between the elements used to form a cohesive text.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: The ability to explain connections between ideas and textual elements was frequently indicative of the level of success.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Common linguistic elements discussed in responses:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Appealed to audience values:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: parochial pride (‘beautiful setting of Timberoona Park’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: social justice (‘offer the chance for some residents to attend … for the first time ever’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: economic responsibility (‘cheaper and smarter investment’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Strategised characterisation (‘highlight of my social calendar’, ‘serious safety and fire risk’, ‘beloved pets’, ‘little Fido or Fluffy’, ‘frantic owners’, ‘children covering their ears and crying’, ‘cheaper and smarter’, ‘romantic kiss’, ‘magic’, ‘safe, spectacular sustainable’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Compared the status quo with an ideal (‘No more … no more …’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Highlighted key values (‘money better spent on …’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Presented a simple solution (‘offers so many advantages’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Used language to escalate emotional responses (‘collective countdown’, ‘a single mistake, a single wayward spark … devastating consequences’, ‘owners desperately searching’, ‘a single environmentally and financially disastrous event that lasts for 10 minutes’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Used language to encourage reflection (‘are these … traditions really dependent on …?’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Used language to inspire (‘we could have actual music’, ‘Together we have the opportunity …’, ‘to lead the way’, ‘spectacular, safe and sustainable’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Highlighted dichotomies (‘do I go out or do I …?’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Used the language of logic (‘this [similar] event was a stunning success …’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Used language to reassure (‘would this be more spectacular …?’)

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Used language to present the solution as the only option (‘a no-brainer’).

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Some strategic visual cues used in the images included:

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: presenting a single relatable pet to represent the cohort

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: encouraging emotional response via the pet’s size and isolation

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: highlighting the pet’s vulnerability and need for an advocate

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: using symbols

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: presenting examples of ‘the magic’ that could be created to inspire and excite.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Students were not expected to laboriously identify and discuss every element. Indeed, to do so within the time constraints would have been impossible. They were expected to select what was most pertinent to the process of persuasion to demonstrate their analytical capacity. The strategic selection of elements central to the persuasion had a high correlation with student success in completing the task. As was the students’ capacity to accept the task’s invitation to discuss the interplay between argument, language and visual elements of the text.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Students needed to first read and understand the text to exercise the skills addressed in this section. Evidence of this capacity has been included in the annotations below.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Assessors read every word the student wrote to determine skill level. The following examples provide insight into the range of skill, not examples of the elements that alone determined the assessment. These are examples of typical skills demonstrated – a single example would never determine a student’s mark.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderDescription: Note: The examples used to demonstrate low skill levels are fabricated examples that demonstrate typical features exhibited at this level. Work of a high-middle and high skill level are examples of actual responses in the examination. Multiple responses at the high skill level have been included to show that there are many ways to demonstrate skill level.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: In Section C, it was apparent that a student’s capacity to use language (EQ3) mirrored other cognitive processes. In desiring to express complex ideas, students naturally developed vocabulary and phrases that enabled communication. Equally, possessing a rich and varied vocabulary enabled students to express their ideas with more precision. However, it must be remembered that the primary function of language is to communicate. In this instance, to communicate how the text was designed to persuade a specific audience. Thus, a student’s time was most wisely spent on that task rather than on merely identifying linguistic elements using polysyllabic nomenclature. As in other sections of the course, the function of language is to communicate relevant ideas. Vocabulary needed to be harnessed purposefully for the task.

Subject: English
SectionHeaderContent: Analysis is a complex cognitive task, and many students were well prepared for the examination.
